4 min read
Just now

--

Fake precision, impossible leaderboards, and the 50 milliseconds nobody talks about — plus what an honest benchmark actually looks like.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by reyna on UnsplashThere’s a whole genre of websites built on a single, irresistible promise: click when the screen turns green, and we’ll tell you how fast your brain is.
Millions of people take these tests. Gamers screenshot their scores. Friends settle arguments with them. And almost all of these sites share the same dirty secret: the number they give you is not what they claim it is.
Lie #1: The precision is fake
When a site tells you that you reacted in “247 ms,” it implies laboratory confidence. But here’s what actually happens between the signal and your click.
The site’s code decides to show green. Your monitor then takes time to physically paint that change — anywhere from a few milliseconds on a fast gaming display to 30+ ms on an average office screen or laptop. You see it, your brain does its work, your finger moves. Then your mouse or touchscreen takes its own sweet time reporting the click back — wireless mice, cheap touchscreens, and browser input handling all add lag.
Add it up and 20–50 milliseconds of every score is your equipment, not your nervous system. On a 250 ms result, that’s up to a fifth of the number. A site that doesn’t tell you this is selling false precision — and nearly none of them tell you.
Worse, many tests don’t even timestamp correctly on their own end. They record when the code requested the color change, not when the frame was actually painted to your screen. That alone can silently pad or shave frames off your score.
Lie #2: The leaderboards are fantasy
Human reactions have a hard floor around 100–120 milliseconds. This is settled physiology — a visual signal needs that long to travel from retina to brain to muscle. It’s why track and field rules automatically flag any sprinter who leaves the blocks under 100 ms as a false start: officials know it’s impossible to genuinely react that fast.
Now go look at the leaderboard of any big reaction-test site. Rows of 40 ms, 60 ms, 80 ms scores. Every one of them is a lucky guess, a script, or a bot. And the site displays them anyway, because a leaderboard full of impossible numbers keeps people clicking, chasing scores that no human being has ever legitimately achieved.
A benchmark that publishes physically impossible results isn’t a benchmark. It’s a slot machine.
Lie #3: One click means something
A single reaction attempt is noise. You might anticipate and get lucky. You might blink and get robbed. Reaction time is a distribution, not a point — and any site that headlines your one best click is measuring your luck, not your speed. The statistically honest move is boring: multiple rounds, take the median, throw away the fluke.
What honesty actually looks like
I’m writing this because a recently launched site called ReactionBench does the opposite of all three, and it’s worth pointing at as the standard the whole genre should be held to.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reaction Time tests on reactionbench.comIt timestamps signals to the exact frame your screen paints them, using the browser’s high-precision clock — and then, remarkably, it admits what it can’t measure. Right on the homepage: your hardware adds roughly 20–50 ms that no web test can see, so don’t treat any browser score as lab-grade. It keeps desktop and mobile results separate, because touchscreen latency makes cross-device comparison unfair. Scores are the median of five rounds, so a lucky click never defines you. And there’s no fake global leaderboard to game — your stats live in your own browser, tracked over time, with no account and nothing uploaded.
It’s also just more of a benchmark: thirteen tests instead of one, covering visual, audio, and choice reactions, impulse control (go/no-go), an F1 lights-out start, a drag-racing tree that fouls you for jumping early, a driving test that converts your reaction into real stopping distance, and a physics-accurate ruler drop straight out of science class.
None of this makes a browser test a research instrument, and the site says so. That’s exactly the point.
Why this matters beyond bragging rights
Reaction time is one of the few cognitive metrics a normal person can measure at home, and it genuinely tracks things that matter — sleep debt, alertness, aging, whether you should really be driving right now. At highway speed, a couple hundred milliseconds of brake reaction is several car lengths of stopping distance.
A measurement that useful deserves to be taken honestly. When sites inflate precision and publish impossible scores, they don’t just flatter gamers — they teach millions of people a wrong intuition about their own bodies.
So the next time a website hands you a suspiciously flattering number, ask the only question that matters for any benchmark, digital or otherwise: did they show their work?
If they didn’t, you didn’t get a measurement. You got a toy.
